name: Lawrencium
name_origin: Named in honor of Ernest O. Lawrence, inventor of the cyclotron and founder of the Lawrence Berkeley Laboratory.

discovery.discoverer: Albert Ghiorso, Torbjorn Sikkeland, Almon Larsh, Robert M. Latimer at Berkeley; with subsequent contributions from JINR Dubna

occurrence.abundance_summary: Synthetic element not found in nature; produced by bombarding californium or einsteinium targets with boron or oxygen ions in heavy-ion accelerators, typically a few atoms per experiment.

compounds.name: Lawrencium(III) chloride
compounds.summary: Inferred trichloride identified only on the tracer scale; chemistry suggests behavior consistent with a heavy actinide(III) more than with group 3 transition metals.

uses: Fundamental research into the closing of the actinide series and the transition into transactinide chemistry
Experimental measurement of the first ionization energy, which provided the first direct test of relativistic predictions for a heavy element
reactivity_summary: Bulk metallic lawrencium has never been isolated. Solution chemistry on the tracer scale shows behavior characteristic of a trivalent heavy actinide; the +3 state is the only one observed. Lawrencium has an unusual ground-state configuration with a 7p1 electron rather than 6d1, due to relativistic stabilization.
isotopes_summary: All isotopes are radioactive. Lawrencium-266 has the longest known half-life (about 11 hours), although the more commonly produced Lr-260 and Lr-256 have half-lives measured in minutes. All isotopes decay by alpha emission or spontaneous fission.

citations.source: IUPAC Periodic Table of the Elements
Sato et al., 'Measurement of the first ionization potential of lawrencium' Nature 520, 209-211 (2015)
